retrieved_knowledge.text: SC02:2026 Business Logic Vulnerabilities. SC02:2026 Business Logic Vulnerabilities. Business logic vulnerabilities describe any situation where a smart contract’s *intended* economic or functional behavior can be subverted even though individual low-level checks (e.g., type safety, reentrancy guards, access control) are correct. These are **design flaws** in how the system’s rules, incentives, state transitions, and invariants are modeled on-chain. Unlike low-level bugs (overflow, reentrancy), business logic flaws arise when the rules themselves are unsafe—the code “does what it says”, but what it says permits exploitable outcomes. This applies across all smart contract domains: DeFi (lending, AMMs, vaults, yield strategies), NFTs (minting logic, royalties, marketplace mechanics), DAOs (voting, delegation, proposal execution), bridges (burn/mint asymmetry, liquidity rules), gaming (reward distribution, fairness), and cross-chain/L2 systems where multi-hop state transitions create emergent vulnerabilities.
retrieved_knowledge.title: SC02:2026 Business Logic Vulnerabilities

retrieved_knowledge.text: SC10:2026 Proxy & Upgradeability Vulnerabilities. SC10:2026 Proxy & Upgradeability Vulnerabilities. Proxy and upgradeability vulnerabilities describe any situation where a smart contract uses an upgradeable architecture (proxy, beacon, or implementation-swapping pattern) and the upgrade path, initialization, or admin controls are misdesigned or misconfigured. Upgradeable contracts separate a **proxy** (which holds state and delegates calls) from an **implementation** (which contains logic). When upgradeability is improperly secured, attackers can hijack the proxy admin or upgrade role to deploy malicious implementations, re-initialize contracts to seize ownership, or bypass critical checks in initialization or migration steps. This affects all contract types that use upgradeability: DeFi (lending, vaults, DEXes), NFTs (collections, marketplaces), DAOs (governance, treasuries), bridges (messengers, asset contracts), and L2/cross-chain systems. Common patterns include Transparent Proxy, UUPS (EIP-1822), Beacon Proxy, and custom router-implementation designs. On non-EVM chains, analogous upgrade mechanisms exist (e.g., Move modules, Solana program upgrades) with similar trust and initialization risks.
retrieved_knowledge.title: SC10:2026 Proxy & Upgradeability Vulnerabilities

retrieved_knowledge.text: Insecure Delegatecall Usage. Insecure Delegatecall Usage. Insecure delegatecall usage refers to vulnerabilities that arise when using `delegatecall` to execute code from another contract. This can lead to: - Unauthorized access to sensitive functions. - Exploitation of vulnerabilities in the called contract. - Loss of funds or data. Category: Execution with Unnecessary Privileges. CWE: CWE-250. SWC: N/A. OWASP: SCWE-035.
retrieved_knowledge.title: Insecure Delegatecall Usage

retrieved_knowledge.text: SC01:2026 Access Control Vulnerabilities. SC01:2026 Access Control Vulnerabilities. Improper access control describes any situation where a smart contract does not rigorously enforce *who* may invoke privileged behavior, *under which* conditions, and *with which* parameters. In modern DeFi systems this goes far beyond a single `onlyOwner` modifier. Governance contracts, multisigs, guardians, proxy admins, and cross‑chain routers all participate in enforcing who can mint or burn tokens, move reserves, reconfigure pools, pause or unpause core logic, or upgrade implementations. If any of these trust boundaries are weak or inconsistently applied, an attacker may be able to impersonate a privileged actor or cause the system to treat an untrusted address as if it were authorized.
retrieved_knowledge.title: SC01:2026 Access Control Vulnerabilities

retrieved_knowledge.text: ERC-20 Token Standard. ERC-20 Token Standard. ERC-20 defines a standard interface for fungible tokens, including token transfers, delegated transfers, allowances, and transfer/approval events. Functions: totalSupply totalSupply(), balanceOf balanceOf(address), transfer transfer(address,uint256), transferFrom transferFrom(address,address,uint256), approve approve(address,uint256), allowance allowance(address,address). Assets: Token balances, Token allowances, Total supply. Security properties: Only a token holder can transfer its own tokens. A spender cannot transfer more than the approved allowance. Transfers preserve balance and total-supply invariants.
retrieved_knowledge.title: ERC-20 Token Standard

retrieved_knowledge.text: ERC-20 approval race condition. ERC-20 approval race condition. A spender exploits transaction ordering around approve() to consume both old and new allowances. Applicable to: ERC-20. Entry points: approve, transferFrom. STRIDE: Tampering. Vulnerability: Transaction-ordering dependent allowance update. Security property: Allowance updates must not allow unintended double spending.. Test objective: Verify that allowance changes cannot be exploited through front-running.. Countermeasure: Use increase/decrease allowance or require resetting allowance to zero before changing it..
retrieved_knowledge.title: ERC-20 approval race condition

retrieved_knowledge.text: Token accounting invariant violation. Token accounting invariant violation. A token operation incorrectly updates balances, ownership, supply, assets, or shares. Applicable to: ERC-20, ERC-721, ERC-1155, ERC-4626. Entry points: transfer, transferFrom, safeTransferFrom, safeBatchTransferFrom, deposit, withdraw, redeem, mint, burn. STRIDE: Tampering. Vulnerability: Incorrect token accounting. Security property: Token operations must preserve standard-specific accounting invariants.. Test objective: Verify that state transitions preserve token accounting invariants.. Countermeasure: Check preconditions and assert post-state accounting invariants..
retrieved_knowledge.title: Token accounting invariant violation

retrieved_knowledge.text: Missing Explicit Function Visibility. Missing Explicit Function Visibility. When a function’s visibility is omitted in Solidity, it defaults to `public`, allowing any caller to invoke logic that may have been intended to be internal or private. This expands the attack surface, enabling unauthorized state changes, fund movements, or reentrancy entry points. Category: Improper Access Control. CWE: CWE-284. SWC: N/A. OWASP: SCWE-097.
retrieved_knowledge.title: Missing Explicit Function Visibility

metadata.source_title: Reentrancy during value or token transfer

retrieved_knowledge.text: Reentrancy during value or token transfer. Reentrancy during value or token transfer. A callback or external value transfer allows re-entry before state is finalized. Applicable to: ERC-20, ERC-721, ERC-1155, ERC-4626. Entry points: withdraw, redeem, safeTransferFrom, safeBatchTransferFrom. STRIDE: Tampering. Vulnerability: Reentrancy. Security property: State must be updated before external calls that may transfer control.. Test objective: Verify that a malicious receiver cannot re-enter and consume state twice.. Countermeasure: Apply checks-effects-interactions and/or a reentrancy guard..
retrieved_knowledge.title: Reentrancy during value or token transfer

metadata.source_title: Lack of Zero Value Check in Token Transfers

retrieved_knowledge.text: Lack of Zero Value Check in Token Transfers. Lack of Zero Value Check in Token Transfers. This weakness occurs when smart contracts perform ERC20 token transfers without first validating that the transfer amount is greater than zero. While most ERC20 tokens allow zero‑value transfers, some implementations (e.g., LEND and others) revert when the amount is zero. This inconsistency can cause unexpected reverts in production, disrupt execution flow, and prevent critical contract logic from completing successfully. Common risks include: - Unexpected revert when interacting with non‑standard ERC20 tokens that disallow zero‑value operations. - Potential denial‑of‑service conditions if an attacker or integration repeatedly triggers zero‑value transfers. - Broken integrations when protocols assume uniform ERC20 behavior across diverse tokens. Category: Improper Check for Unusual or Exceptional Conditions. CWE: CWE-754. SWC: N/A. OWASP: SCWE-091.
retrieved_knowledge.title: Lack of Zero Value Check in Token Transfers

retrieved_knowledge.text: NIST SP 800-53 mitigation guidance. NIST SP 800-53 mitigation guidance. Auxiliary control catalog used to enrich generated countermeasures with governance, monitoring, access control, audit, and configuration-management guidance. Role: mitigation_guidance. Applies to: access control, audit logging, configuration management, incident response, monitoring, risk assessment. Fields: . Mappings: Access Control Unauthorized privileged action Enrich countermeasures with least privilege, role separation, and privileged action review. System and Information Integrity Oracle manipulation Enrich countermeasures with monitoring, validation, and integrity checks on external data sources. Risk Assessment and Audit Governance manipulation Enrich countermeasures with auditability, approval workflows, and change-management controls.. Usage guidelines: Use NIST as mitigation guidance, not as an ERC vulnerability source. Attach NIST control areas to countermeasures when useful for reporting. Keep smart-contract-specific mitigations from OWASP, EthTrust, and OpenZeppelin as primary technical controls..
retrieved_knowledge.title: NIST SP 800-53 mitigation guidance

retrieved_knowledge.text: CVSS. CVSS. Standardized scoring model for assessing the severity of known vulnerabilities using metrics such as attack vector, complexity, privileges, user interaction, scope, and CIA impact. Role: vulnerability_scoring. Applies to: known vulnerabilities, deployed smart contract weaknesses, security reporting. Fields: How the vulnerability is exploited. attack_vector Conditions beyond attacker control required for exploitation. attack_complexity Privileges required before exploitation. privileges_required Whether another user must participate. user_interaction Impact on confidentiality. confidentiality Impact on integrity. integrity Impact on availability. availability. Mappings: . Usage guidelines: Use CVSS when a threat is grounded in a concrete vulnerability. Adapt metric interpretation carefully for blockchain execution semantics. Keep the vector string and rationale in the generated model..
retrieved_knowledge.title: CVSS

metadata.source_title: Please note, this content is no longer actively maintained.

retrieved_knowledge.text: Please note, this content is no longer actively maintained.. Please note, this content is no longer actively maintained.. Functions that do not have a function visibility type specified are `public` by default. This can lead to a vulnerability if a developer forgot to set the visibility and a malicious user is able to make unauthorized or unintended state changes. Category: Improper Adherence to Coding Standards. CWE: CWE-710. SWC: SWC-100. OWASP: N/A.
retrieved_knowledge.title: Please note, this content is no longer actively maintained.

retrieved_knowledge.text: Missing Supply Cap Enforcement. Missing Supply Cap Enforcement. Tokens or lending systems without hard supply caps allow privileged accounts or flawed logic to mint unlimited units, inflating supply and draining collateralized protocols. Lack of caps also breaks price assumptions in AMMs and oracles. Category: Protection Mechanism Failure. CWE: CWE-693. SWC: N/A. OWASP: SCWE-116.
retrieved_knowledge.title: Missing Supply Cap Enforcement

retrieved_knowledge.text: SC04:2026 Flash Loan–Facilitated Attacks. SC04:2026 Flash Loan–Facilitated Attacks. Flash loan–facilitated attacks describe exploits where an attacker uses **uncollateralized, same-transaction borrowing** (flash loans) to amplify an underlying vulnerability into a profitable, protocol-draining attack. Flash loans are not inherently vulnerable—they are a legitimate DeFi primitive—but they grant attackers arbitrarily large, transient capital within a single transaction. Any protocol that makes trust assumptions based on *capital at risk* or *historical position size* is exposed when an attacker can temporarily hold massive balances without putting their own funds at risk. This affects all contract types that assume economic constraints or proportional exposure: lending protocols (governance vote weight, liquidation thresholds, collateral ratios), AMMs (share minting, arbitrage, oracle skew), yield vaults (deposit/withdraw/share accounting), governance (vote buying, flash-loan governance attacks), NFT and token valuation (floor price, collateral valuation), and cross-protocol composability where one contract’s state is influenced by another’s liquidity. On non-EVM chains, similar concepts exist (e.g., transient large positions within a single block or batch).
retrieved_knowledge.title: SC04:2026 Flash Loan–Facilitated Attacks

metadata.source_title: MITRE ATT&CK for blockchain threat contextualization

retrieved_knowledge.text: MITRE ATT&CK for blockchain threat contextualization. MITRE ATT&CK for blockchain threat contextualization. Maps generated blockchain and ERC threats to adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures when the threat involves attacker behavior beyond a pure smart-contract weakness. Role: contextualization. Applies to: oracle manipulation, bridge compromise, wallet compromise, governance manipulation, off-chain infrastructure compromise, DEX manipulation. Fields: . Mappings: Data manipulation or compromise of trusted external data sources. Tampering Oracle manipulation Attach as auxiliary context to the generated threat rationale and attack scenario. Abuse of valid permissions, compromised accounts, or manipulation of decision-making workflows. Elevation of Privilege Governance manipulation Attach as auxiliary context when attack steps include off-chain compromise or social coordination. Credential or key compromise enabling an attacker to act as a legitimate user. Spoofing Wallet or private-key compromise Attach as auxiliary context when the smart contract threat depends on account compromise.. Usage guidelines: Do not use MITRE ATT&CK as the primary source for ERC-specific vulnerabilities. Use it to enrich attack scenarios and attack trees with real-world adversary behavior. Prefer ERC, OWASP, SWC, CWE, and EthTrust for identifying smart-contract weaknesses..
retrieved_knowledge.title: MITRE ATT&CK for blockchain threat contextualization

countermeasures.description: Use increase/decrease allowance or require resetting allowance to zero before changing it.
Preserve balance and total-supply invariants across transfer, mint, and burn operations.
Restrict minting to the owner or an authorized minter role.
Update state before external calls and protect sensitive functions with a reentrancy guard.

securityProperties.description: Allowance updates must not allow unintended double spending.
Token operations must preserve balances and total-supply invariants.
Only authorized actors can mint new tokens.
External callbacks must not allow repeated consumption of the same state.

testObjectives.expectedBehavior: approve() cannot be exploited to spend both old and new allowances.
transfer, mint, and burn operations preserve expected token accounting.
mint() reverts when called by an unauthorized actor.
withdraw() cannot be re-entered to drain funds.
